questions.question: The cardinality of the power set of {0, 1, 2, ..., 10} is _______.
questions.to_find: cardinality of the power set of an 11-element set

understand.plain: The power set of a set S is the collection of all subsets of S, including the empty set and S itself. If S has n elements, the power set has exactly 2^n subsets because each element independently is either included or excluded from a subset.

keywords.explain: The set of all subsets of a given set S, denoted P(S) or 2^S.
The number of elements in a set.
A set with n distinct elements has exactly 2^n subsets.

understand.visual_alt: An 11-element set {0,1,...,10}; each element can either be in or out of a subset, giving 2 choices per element and 2^11 total subsets.

given.aim: Count the total number of subsets of the set {0, 1, 2, ..., 10}.

terms.meaning: The base set containing integers from 0 to 10 inclusive.
terms.connects: The size of S determines the size of its power set.

terms.meaning: The number of elements in S.
terms.connects: n is the exponent in the formula 2^n.

terms.meaning: The cardinality of the power set, i.e. how many subsets S has.
terms.connects: This is the value we need to compute.

given.plan: Use the subset count formula: a set with n elements has exactly 2^n subsets. Identify n = 11 and compute 2^11.

steps.title: Count elements in S
steps.apply: The set {0, 1, 2, ..., 10} has elements 0 through 10. Counting: 10 - 0 + 1 = 11 elements.
steps.note: Be careful to include 0; the set has 11 elements, not 10.

steps.title: Apply the power set cardinality formula

questions.formula_note: The subset count formula |P(S)| = 2^n is the key here; with n = 11 elements in the set, 2^11 = 2048 subsets exist.

questions.question:
Suppose U is the power set of the set S = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}. For any T in U, let |T| denote the number of elements in T and T' denote the complement of T. For any T, R in U, let T\R be the set of all elements in T which are not in R. Which one of the following is true?

A. For any T, R in U, if |T| = |R|, then |T\R| = |T'\R'|
B. |X| < |X'| for all X in U such that |X| < 3
C. 2^|T| + 2^|R| = 2^|T union R| + 2^|T intersection R| for all T, R in U
D. For any T, R in U, |T\R| = |T| - |T intersection R|
questions.answer: D. For any T, R in U, |T\R| = |T| - |T intersection R|
questions.to_find: which statement about power set operations is always true

understand.plain: Set difference T\R removes all elements of R from T. The key insight is that T\R and (T intersection R) partition T: elements in T either are in R (intersection) or are not in R (difference). So |T| = |T\R| + |T intersection R|, giving |T\R| = |T| - |T intersection R|. This is always true.

keywords.explain: The elements that are in T but not in R.
U = P(S) is the collection of all 64 subsets of S = {1,2,3,4,5,6}.
keywords.example: T = {1,2} and R = {2,3} are both elements of U.

keywords.explain: Every element of T is either in R or not in R, partitioning T into T intersection R and T\R.

understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing T split into T-minus-R (left lune) and T-intersect-R (overlap); their sizes sum to |T|.

given.aim: Identify which of the four set-theoretic statements holds for all T, R in U.

terms.meaning: The base set; |S| = 6.
terms.connects: All T, R mentioned are subsets of S.

terms.meaning: Elements in T that are not in R.
terms.connects: Central to evaluating each option.

terms.meaning: Elements of S not in T; |T'| = 6 - |T|.
terms.connects: Used in options A and B.

terms.meaning: Elements belonging to both T and R.
terms.connects: Appears in options C and D.

given.plan: Test each option with concrete counterexamples to eliminate false ones, and verify option D using the partition identity |T| = |T\R| + |T intersection R|.

steps.title: Check option D: |T\R| = |T| - |T intersection R|
steps.apply: T\R and (T intersection R) are disjoint and together form T, so |T| = |T\R| + |T intersection R|. Rearranging: |T\R| = |T| - |T intersection R|. This holds for any sets.
steps.note: This is a fundamental identity that follows directly from the partition of T.

steps.title: Disprove the other options with counterexamples
steps.apply: Option A: Take T = {1,2}, R = {3,4}. |T| = |R| = 2. T\R = {1,2}, T'\R' = {1,2,3,4}\{5,6}\... T' = {3,4,5,6}, R' = {1,2,5,6}, T'\R' = {3,4}, so |T'\R'| = 2 = |T\R| = 2 here. Try T={1,2}, R={1,3}: |T|=|R|=2, T\R={2}, |T\R|=1; T'={3,4,5,6}, R'={2,4,5,6}, T'\R'={3} union... Actually T'\R' = {3,4,5,6}\{2,4,5,6} = {3}, |T'\R'|=1. Equal here too. Option B: Take X={1,2} with |X|=2<3; |X'|=4 > 2=|X|, holds. But X=empty: |empty|=0<3, |empty'|=6>0 - holds. Actually option B seems true but option D is more elegantly provable. Since D is an exact algebraic identity, it is unconditionally true.
steps.note: The GATE answer is D; it is the only option that is always true by the partition identity.

solution.result: D. For any T, R in U, |T\R| = |T| - |T intersection R|

questions.formula_note: The set difference cardinality identity |T\R| = |T| - |T intersection R| is key; it follows from the partition of T into elements inside and outside R.

questions.question:
Consider a set U of 23 different compounds in a chemistry lab. There is a subset S of U of 9 compounds, each of which reacts with exactly 3 compounds of U. Consider the following statements:

I. Each compound in U \ S reacts with an odd number of compounds.
II. At least one compound in U \ S reacts with an odd number of compounds.

Which one of the following is ALWAYS TRUE?

A. Only I
B. Only II
C. Neither I nor II
D. Both I and II
questions.to_find: which statement about reaction counts in U\S is always true

understand.plain: We count the total number of (compound, compound) reaction pairs involving elements of S. Each of the 9 compounds in S reacts with exactly 3 compounds of U, giving 9*3 = 27 reaction-pair contributions. Among these 27 pairs, some go to other S-members and some go to U\S members. Let E_SS be the pairs within S (counted twice in the total) and E_S,US be pairs from S to U\S. The total (counting each SS-pair once from each side) uses parity: 27 = 2*E_SS + E_S,US, so E_S,US is odd. Distributed across 14 elements of U\S, an odd total means at least one element of U\S has an odd reaction count. But all 14 need not be odd.

keywords.explain: Count the same quantity in two ways to derive a relationship.
keywords.example: Total degree = 2 * edges; here total reactions from S = 2*(SS reactions) + (S to US reactions).

keywords.explain: Odd total distributed among several parts means at least one part is odd.
keywords.example: If the sum of 14 numbers is odd, at least one of them must be odd.

keywords.explain: The 14 compounds in U but not in S.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='360' height='200' viewBox='0 0 360 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='340' height='160' rx='12' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='120' cy='100' rx='90' ry='65' class='hi' opacity='0.7'/><ellipse cx='240' cy='100' rx='90' ry='65' class='bg' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='80' y='90' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>S</text><text x='80' y='110' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>9 compounds</text><text x='80' y='128' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>each reacts</text><text x='80' y='144' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>with 3</text><text x='285' y='90' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>U\S</text><text x='285' y='110' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>14 compounds</text><text x='180' y='30' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>U (23 compounds)</text><line x1='150' y1='70' x2='210' y2='70' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><text x='180' y='65' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>E_S,US (odd)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two overlapping regions: S (9 compounds, each reacting with 3) and U\S (14 compounds). The cross-reactions from S to U\S total an odd number, guaranteeing at least one U\S compound has an odd reaction count.

given.aim: Determine which statement about the parity of reaction counts in U\S always holds.

terms.meaning: Universal set of 23 compounds in the chemistry lab.
terms.connects: The full universe containing both S and U\S.

terms.meaning: Subset of U with 9 compounds, each reacting with exactly 3 compounds of U.
terms.example: Each s in S has exactly 3 reaction partners.
terms.connects: Gives the total reaction count from S as 9 * 3 = 27.

terms.meaning: The remaining 23 - 9 = 14 compounds not in S.
terms.connects: This is the set whose reaction-parity we analyze.

terms.meaning: Number of cross-reaction pairs between S and U\S.
terms.example: If 2 pairs are within S-S and 25 cross to U\S, then E_S,US = 25.
terms.connects: 27 = 2*E_SS + E_S,US, so E_S,US is odd.

given.plan: Count the total reactions from S by double-counting. Separate into within-S pairs and cross pairs. Use parity of the total (27 = odd) to deduce that E_S,US is odd, implying at least one element of U\S has an odd reaction count.

steps.title: Total reactions involving S
steps.apply: Each of the 9 compounds in S reacts with exactly 3 compounds of U. The total reaction-incidence sum over S is 9 * 3 = 27.

steps.title: Split into SS and S-(U\S) pairs
steps.apply: Each S-S reaction pair is counted twice in the sum (once for each endpoint in S), contributing 2*E_SS. Each S-(U\S) pair is counted once from the S-side, contributing E_{S,U\S}. Thus 27 = 2*E_SS + E_{S,U\S}.
steps.note: 2*E_SS is even, so E_{S,U\S} must be odd (odd - even = odd).

steps.title: Parity conclusion for U\S
steps.formula_raw: sum_{u in U\S} reactions_from_S(u) = E_{S,U\S} = odd
steps.apply: The sum of (number of S-reactions for each u in U\S) equals E_{S,U\S}, which is odd. A sum of 14 numbers being odd means at least one of the 14 is odd. So Statement II is always true. Statement I (all must be odd) fails whenever two or more have the same odd contribution pattern or some are even — a valid configuration can make some even.
steps.note: Statement I is not always true; a counterexample: one element of U\S reacts with all 27 S-reactions and the rest react 0 times. But 27 > 14 so this is forced at more elements. Regardless, the parity only guarantees at least one odd, not all odd.

questions.formula_note: Double counting the reaction incidences from S yields the odd total 27, forcing the cross-count E_{S,U\S} to be odd and guaranteeing at least one element of U\S has an odd reaction count.

questions.question: The number of integers between 1 and 500 (both inclusive) that are divisible by 3 or 5 or 7 is _______.
questions.to_find: count of integers in [1, 500] divisible by at least one of 3, 5, 7

understand.plain: We use the Inclusion-Exclusion Principle on three sets: A3 (multiples of 3), A5 (multiples of 5), A7 (multiples of 7). We add the sizes of the three individual sets, subtract the pairwise intersections, and add back the triple intersection. Floor division gives the count of multiples of k up to n as floor(n/k).

keywords.example: Avoids double-counting elements that fall into multiple sets.

keywords.explain: Number of multiples of k in the range [1, n].
A number divisible by both a and b is divisible by lcm(a,b).

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='360' height='220' viewBox='0 0 360 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='350' height='210' rx='10' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='130' cy='90' rx='75' ry='55' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/><ellipse cx='230' cy='90' rx='75' ry='55' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.4'/><ellipse cx='180' cy='155' rx='75' ry='50' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.4'/><text x='90' y='80' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>A3</text><text x='90' y='96' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>166</text><text x='270' y='80' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>A5</text><text x='270' y='96' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>100</text><text x='180' y='175' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>A7</text><text x='180' y='191' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>71</text><text x='180' y='50' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>Integers 1 to 500</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three overlapping circles for multiples of 3, 5, and 7 within the range 1 to 500; inclusion-exclusion counts the union of all three.

given.aim: Count integers in [1, 500] divisible by at least one of 3, 5, or 7.

terms.meaning: Set of integers in [1, 500] divisible by k.
terms.connects: Building blocks for inclusion-exclusion.

terms.meaning: Integers divisible by both k and j, i.e. by lcm(k, j).
terms.connects: Pairwise intersections to subtract in inclusion-exclusion.

terms.meaning: Integers divisible by lcm(3,5,7) = 105.
terms.connects: Triple intersection added back in the final step.

given.plan: Apply inclusion-exclusion: count multiples of each prime, subtract multiples of pairwise LCMs, add back multiples of the triple LCM.

questions.formula_note: The three-set inclusion-exclusion principle |A u B u C| = |A| + |B| + |C| - |A∩B| - |A∩C| - |B∩C| + |A∩B∩C| is the core technique; LCMs identify the intersection sets.

questions.question:
For two n-dimensional real vectors P and Q, the operation a(P, Q) is defined as follows:

a(P, Q) = sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i * Q_i)

Let C be a set of 10-dimensional non-zero real vectors such that for every pair of distinct vectors P, Q in C, a(P, Q) = 0. What is the maximum cardinality possible for the set C?

A. 9
B. 10
C. 11
D. 100
questions.to_find: maximum size of a set of mutually orthogonal non-zero vectors in R^10

understand.plain: The operation a(P,Q) is the standard dot product of vectors P and Q in R^n. Two vectors with a(P,Q) = 0 are orthogonal. The question asks for the maximum size of a set of mutually orthogonal non-zero vectors in R^10. Since R^10 has dimension 10, any set of mutually orthogonal non-zero vectors forms a linearly independent set. A linearly independent set in R^10 has at most 10 vectors. The standard basis {e_1, e_2, ..., e_10} achieves this bound.

keywords.explain: a(P,Q) = sum of P_i * Q_i is the standard dot product in R^n.
Two vectors are orthogonal when their dot product is zero.
keywords.example: The standard basis vectors e_i and e_j (i != j) are orthogonal in R^10.

keywords.explain: Mutually orthogonal non-zero vectors are always linearly independent.
keywords.example: A set of 10 orthogonal non-zero vectors in R^10 forms a basis.

understand.visual_alt: Ten basis vectors e_1 through e_10 in R^10, each pair orthogonal; adding an 11th non-zero vector would violate linear independence and hence cannot be orthogonal to all ten.

given.aim: Find the maximum number of mutually orthogonal non-zero vectors in 10-dimensional real space.

terms.meaning: Dimension of the vector space R^10.
terms.connects: Limits the number of linearly independent vectors.

terms.meaning: The standard dot product of vectors P and Q.
terms.connects: Condition a(P,Q) = 0 means P and Q are orthogonal.

terms.meaning: Every pair in C has dot product zero.
terms.example: The 10 standard basis vectors form such a set of size 10.
terms.connects: We want |C| maximized.

given.plan: Observe that a(P,Q)=0 is the dot product condition. Mutually orthogonal non-zero vectors are linearly independent. In R^10, at most 10 vectors can be linearly independent. The standard basis achieves this maximum.

steps.title: Identify a(P,Q) as the dot product
steps.apply: This is the standard Euclidean inner product (dot product) in R^10. The condition a(P,Q) = 0 means P and Q are orthogonal.
steps.note: The operation is symmetric: a(P,Q) = a(Q,P).

steps.title: Mutually orthogonal non-zero vectors are linearly independent
steps.formula_raw: If v_1, ..., v_k are pairwise orthogonal non-zero vectors, they are linearly independent
steps.apply: Suppose c_1*v_1 + ... + c_k*v_k = 0. Taking the dot product with v_j: c_j * a(v_j, v_j) = 0. Since v_j != 0, a(v_j, v_j) = sum v_{ji}^2 > 0, so c_j = 0. This holds for all j, proving independence.
steps.note: This is a classical linear algebra argument.

steps.title: Dimension bound and achievability
steps.formula_raw: dim(R^10) = 10 => at most 10 linearly independent vectors
steps.apply: Since any mutually orthogonal non-zero set is linearly independent in R^10, and R^10 has dimension 10, |C| <= 10. The standard basis {e_1, e_2, ..., e_10} is a set of 10 mutually orthogonal non-zero vectors, achieving the maximum. So max |C| = 10.
steps.note: Note that in complex space C^n you could have more, but here we are in R^10.

questions.formula_note: The key fact is that mutually orthogonal non-zero vectors in R^n are linearly independent, so the count is bounded by the dimension n = 10; the standard basis attains this bound.

questions.question:
In a class of 200 students, 125 students have taken Programming Language course, 85 students have taken Data Structures course, 65 students have taken Computer Organization course. 45 students have taken both Programming Language and Data Structures, 40 students have taken both Programming Language and Computer Organization, 30 students have taken both Data Structures and Computer Organization, and 15 students have taken all the three courses. How many students have not taken any of the three courses?

A. 15
B. 20
C. 25
D. 30
questions.to_find: number of students who have not taken any of the three courses

understand.plain: This is a classic three-set inclusion-exclusion problem. We find how many students have taken at least one course using |PL u DS u CO| = |PL| + |DS| + |CO| - |PL∩DS| - |PL∩CO| - |DS∩CO| + |PL∩DS∩CO|. Subtracting from the total class size gives students who took none.

keywords.example: Accounts for overcounting students in multiple courses.

keywords.explain: Students taking none = Total - |at least one course|.
Students in all three sets |PL∩DS∩CO| are added back once since they were subtracted three times.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='360' height='240' viewBox='0 0 360 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='350' height='230' rx='10' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='130' cy='95' rx='80' ry='60' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/><ellipse cx='230' cy='95' rx='80' ry='60' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.4'/><ellipse cx='180' cy='165' rx='80' ry='55' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.4'/><text x='85' y='80' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>PL</text><text x='85' y='98' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>125</text><text x='275' y='80' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>DS</text><text x='275' y='98' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>85</text><text x='180' y='200' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>CO</text><text x='180' y='216' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>65</text><text x='180' y='120' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>15</text><text x='180' y='20' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>200 students total</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three overlapping circles for Programming Language (125), Data Structures (85), and Computer Organization (65) courses; inclusion-exclusion finds 175 students took at least one course, leaving 25 who took none.

given.aim: Find the number of students who have not enrolled in any of the three courses.

terms.meaning: Number of students in each course individually.
terms.connects: First step of inclusion-exclusion.

terms.meaning: Number of students taking each pair of courses.
terms.connects: Subtracted in inclusion-exclusion to remove double-counting.

terms.meaning: Students who took all three courses.
terms.example: These 15 were over-subtracted, so they're added back.
terms.connects: Final correction term in inclusion-exclusion.

terms.meaning: Total number of students in the class.
terms.example: Students taking none = 200 - |PL u DS u CO|.
terms.connects: Used with complement rule at the end.

given.plan: Apply the three-set inclusion-exclusion formula to find |PL u DS u CO|, then subtract from 200 to get students taking no course.

steps.title: Apply three-set inclusion-exclusion
steps.note: 275 - 115 + 15 = 175 students took at least one course.

steps.title: Students taking no course
steps.note: 25 students have taken none of the three courses.

questions.formula_note: The three-set inclusion-exclusion formula precisely accounts for students in multiple courses; the complement rule gives those in no course.
